# Forcing Transitive Versions via Dependency Management
*or what to Do When a Transitive Dependency is the Wrong Version*

This scenario demonstrates how to force a specific version of a transitive dependency, overriding what the intermediate libraries request.

#### Dependency Graph
```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 4.4
graph LR
    Root[Project Root] --> LibA[lib-a:1.0.0]
    LibA --> LibB[lib-b:1.0.0]
    LibB --> LibC[lib-c:1.0.0]
    Root -- forces --> LibC_Forced[lib-c:2.0.0]
```

#### Maven (pom.xml)

```{.xml include="/demos/maven-demo/scenario-6-forcing/pom.xml" snippet="s6-deps"}
```

#### Expected Intuition
"I said use 2.0.0, so use 2.0.0."

#### Actual Resolution Results
Using `<dependencyManagement>`, `lib-c` becomes `2.0.0`.

#### Classpath Reality
`lib-c-2.0.0.jar` is on the classpath.

#### Why This Happens
This scenario uses the exact same `<dependencyManagement>` mechanism as Scenario 3, but applies it to a *transitive* dependency instead of a direct one. There is no special "forcing" keyword in Maven; it's all handled by `<dependencyManagement>`. It works because Maven evaluates the `<dependencyManagement>` section after building the complete dependency graph but *before* selecting the final versions. This allows the managed versions to override any version chosen by the "nearest-wins" algorithm.

#### How Developers Commonly "Fix" This
This is often the fix for "Nearest Wins" picking an old version, or to patch a vulnerability deep in the graph.

#### Safer Ways to Take Control
This is a safe and recommended pattern for enforcing policy.

#### Signals to Watch For
*   If you force a version that is too new, `lib-b` might break (binary incompatibility).

#### How This Scales in Real Systems
Strict constraints are vital for security compliance (e.g., "Must use OpenSSL >= 3.0").

#### Key Takeaway
`<dependencyManagement>` overrides transitive version resolution (nearest-wins), making it the primary tool for enforcing version policy across the dependency tree. Note that a direct version in `<dependencies>` will still take precedence (see Scenario 4).

#### Related Scenarios
*   [Scenario 3: Dependency Management (BOM)](../scenario-3-mgmt/guide.qmd) - The core mechanism used here.
*   [Scenario 4: Hardcoded Version vs Dependency Management](../scenario-4-override/guide.qmd) - Understanding the precedence of direct declarations.
*   [Scenario 14: Reject](../scenario-14-reject/guide.qmd) - Forcing *away* from a version.
